This is likely a formatting issue where the equation was lost during OCR extraction. The context describes it as a histogram over cluster assignments, but without the explicit formula, a reader cannot confirm the exact method (e.g., whether counts are normalized by total samples, or if additive smoothing is applied).

comments.title: Reference to non-existent figure or unshown part of Figure 2
comments.quote: Figure 2: Divergence curves for different models (GPT-2 [45], Grover [61]) and decoding algorithms (greedy decoding, ancestral and nucleus sampling).
comments.explanation: Figure 2 is referenced in the passage (in Section 2) and described in detail, but its actual image is not included in the provided text. The passage discusses the Figure's content (e.g., 'Rightmost: Nucleus sampling has a slightly smaller Type I error...') which assumes the reader can see the figure. This is a missing figure issue rather than an error in the text itself, but it prevents the reader from verifying the claims made about the divergence curves.

comments.title: Unclear definition of divergence curve coordinates in prose
comments.quote: a coordinate and a similarly defined coordinate KL( _Q|Rλ_ ).

comments.title: Misstatement of relationship between KL divergences and Type I/II errors at the mixture
comments.quote: In particular, we define the (soft) Type I error at level λ as KL(Q|Rλ) and the ( (soft) Type II error as KL(P |Rλ).
comments.explanation: Initially, I read the definition and found it plausible. However, upon re-examining the earlier text (page 2, the two bullet points defining Type I and Type II errors), I noticed a subtle inconsistency. The text first says: '(Type I) Q places high mass on text which is unlikely under P' and '(Type II) Q cannot generate text which is plausible under P'. The formal KL definitions given just before the soft versions are: KL(Q||P) for Type I and KL(P||Q) for Type II. Intuitively, the 'soft' version should keep the same direction: the measure of Type I error should be a divergence from Q to something close to P (i.e., KL(Q||Rλ) since Rλ is a mixture that includes P), and Type II should be from P to something close to Q (i.e., KL(P||Rλ)). The text's assignment (KL(Q|Rλ) for Type I, KL(P|Rλ) for Type II) is, upon reflection, correct given this intended correspondence. However, the prose that immediately precedes this definition states that the 'soft' version replaces the full target distribution with a mixture, but does not clearly explain why the direction of the KL for Type I and Type II is preserved rather than swapped. A careful reader might momentarily expect Type I to be measured by how much Q's mass is 'unexpected' under the mixture (KL(Q||Rλ)) and Type II by how much P's mass is 'unexpected' under the mixture (KL(P||Rλ)), which surprisingly matches the text. So this is not an error. The issue is minor because the definition is consistent, but the justification for why these particular soft divergences correspond to the original error types is glossed over, which could cause brief confusion.

comments.title: OCR misread in the definition of the divergence curve coordinate
comments.quote: Each point on the divergence curve C(P, Q) consists of a coordinate and a similarly defined coordinate KL(Q|Rλ).
comments.explanation: The text says 'Each point on the divergence curve C(P, Q) consists of a coordinate' but then cuts off without showing the actual expression. Given the surrounding context (the equation is introduced but not rendered in the OCR-extracted passage), this appears to be a formatting/OCR error where the formula for the first coordinate (presumably involving KL(P||Rλ) or a scaled version) is missing. The passage also mentions a scaling hyperparameter 'c > 0' in the earlier definition of the divergence curve but does not repeat it here. However, since the equation is shown in the earlier part of the passage (though partially garbled) and the key idea is still understandable, this is not a real technical error because the surrounding text clarifies that the curve consists of two KL divergences. The omission does not undermine the scientific content.

comments.title: Equation (2) referenced but not present in extracted text
comments.quote: We cannot compute the summation as written in Eq. (2),
comments.explanation: The text references 'Eq. (2)' for a summation that cannot be computed, but the equation itself is not included in the visible OCR excerpt. This is likely due to the PDF extraction boundary, not a mathematical error. The surrounding prose makes it sufficiently clear that the issue is the need for ground-truth probabilities P(·) and the large support size. Since this is a forward reference to an equation that presumably appears in the original full paper but is lost in this excerpt, it does not constitute an error in reasoning or mathematics. It is merely a missing visual element.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the claim that generation perplexity can be derived from a single point inside the divergence curve
comments.quote: In fact, we show in Appendix A that the generation perplexity can be derived from a single point enclosed between the divergence curve and the axes.
comments.explanation: The text makes a strong claim about the relationship between generation perplexity and the divergence curve, citing Appendix A for the proof. However, the claim assumes that the reader will accept that the divergence curve encloses a region that contains generation perplexity as a single point. The connection between generation perplexity (which involves expected log-probabilities under an external model) and the KL-based divergence curve is not immediately obvious. The authors do not provide even a sketch of the reasoning here, and the appendix is not available for review. While this is acceptable for a conference paper that has space constraints, a careful reader may question whether the claim is truly justified or whether it overstates the relationship. Nonetheless, the presence of the appendix reference means the claim is not unsubstantiated—it merely requires trusting the Appendix. I will flag this as moderate because the claim is central to the motivation of MAUVE vs. other metrics, and without the derivation, the reader cannot assess its validity.

comments.title: Confusing use of 'adversarial perplexity sampling' in section 4 without formal definition
comments.quote: We also consider an adversarial sampling procedure, designed to generate low-quality text that nevertheless matches the perplexity of human text. Adversarial perplexity sampling proceeds in two phases: (1) we generate the first 15% of tokens in a sequence uniformly at random from the vocabulary, and (2) we generate the remaining tokens greedily to make the running perplexity of the generated sequence as close as possible to the perplexity of human text.
comments.explanation: The description of this adversarial procedure is underspecified. How exactly is 'making the running perplexity as close as possible to the perplexity of human text' implemented algorithmically? Is there a search over token choices? A closed-form decision rule? How is the target 'perplexity of human text' computed (e.g., on the training set, on the context, on the entire dataset)? The procedure as described is not reproducible. The authors do not specify what base model is used to compute perplexity during generation (e.g., is it the same GPT-2 model?), nor do they explain how the greedy phase adjusts tokens to match a target perplexity. This matters because later empirical results likely depend on this algorithm, and without clarity, the reader cannot assess the validity of the comparisons. This is a moderate issue because it concerns the reproducibility of an experimental method, but it does not affect the core mathematical framework of MAUVE.

comments.title: Generation perplexity expression appears to have a formatting error
comments.quote: Generation Perplexity | Generation quality via external |_|E_Q_[log_R_(**_x_**)]_−_E_P_[log_R_(**_x_**)]_|_ | [18] | model_R_ | (a single point inside_C_(_P, Q_))
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the row for 'Generation Perplexity' contains what looks like an OCR artifact: the mathematical expression for the measure is garbled with underscores and pipe characters. The intended expression seems to be 'E_Q[log R(x)] - E_P[log R(x)]', but the formatting makes it ambiguous. However, the surrounding context (the description of Generation Perplexity in §3) clarifies that it compares perplexity of model text and human text under an external model R, so the general meaning is recoverable. Given this is a table formatting issue from OCR and the meaning is clear from the prose, I would not flag it as a real issue for the paper's content, but the garbled formula could confuse a reader relying solely on the table.

comments.title: Underspecified definition of divergence curve scaling hyperparameter c
comments.quote: where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter for scaling.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a hyperparameter c > 0 in the definition of the divergence curve C(P,Q) but does not specify where c appears in the curve's formula. The definition earlier states 'C(P,Q) = { (KL(P||R_λ), KL(Q||R_λ)) : λ ∈ [0,1] } with scaling hyperparameter c > 0', but the scaling is not shown in the coordinate expressions. The context (including the summary) notes c is part of the curve definition, but its precise role (e.g., whether it weights the KL terms or rescales axes) is left ambiguous. A careful reader might wonder if c modifies the mixture R_λ or the KL divergence itself. This underspecification could impede reproduction, though the empirical results in §4.2 claim robustness to scaling, so the exact role may be secondary.

comments.title: Minor inconsistency in reported number of generations for News task
comments.quote: News | Grover (all sizes) | Pretrained | RealNews | varying | 1024tokens | 5000
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the 'Prompt Length' for News is listed as 'varying', but the text in §4 describes the task as a text completion task where each domain has a context and continuation. For web text and stories, specific prompt lengths are given (35 and 50 tokens respectively). The 'varying' prompt length for News is not further explained; it could mean different contexts have different lengths, or that the prompt length is determined by the dataset. While this is not a mathematical error, it is an underspecified method detail that could affect reproducibility if a reader wanted to exactly replicate the experimental setup.

comments.title: Potentially misleading claim that HUSE cannot handle sparse Q
comments.quote: As a separate technical caveat, it is unclear how to use HUSE for sparse _Q_ that assigns zero probability to a subset of text, which is the case with state-of-the-art decoding algorithms [26, 39].
comments.explanation: The text claims HUSE (Human-Unique Statistical Evaluation) is unclear for sparse Q because HUSE uses perplexity under Q for Type II errors, and sparse Q may assign zero probability to some text. However, HUSE could in principle handle zero probabilities by using a smoothed or modified perplexity (e.g., additive smoothing), as the authors themselves note for other metrics like ε-perplexity. The paper does not discuss whether the original HUSE authors addressed this or whether adaptation is straightforward. The claim may overstate the limitation, though it is not necessarily false—it depends on the specific implementation. This is a minor overstatement rather than a major error.

comments.title: Underspecified adversarial sampling procedure
comments.quote: Adversarial perplexity sampling proceeds in two phases: (1) we generate the first 15% of tokens in a sequence uniformly at random from the vocabulary, and (2) we generate the remaining tokens greedily to make the running perplexity of the generated sequence as close as possible to the perplexity of human text.
comments.explanation: The description is too vague for reproducibility. Key details are missing: (a) 'running perplexity' is not defined—does it refer to the perplexity of the entire generated sequence so far under the same model or an external model? (b) 'as close as possible' is ambiguous—how is the greedy token selection performed to achieve this? (c) 'perplexity of human text' is not anchored—it could be the average perplexity of the human continuations in the dataset, or a specific reference value. Without these details, a reader cannot implement the procedure. The method plays a role in the evaluation (Table 3), so this imprecision weakens the empirical contribution.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency for greedy decoding MAUVE value
comments.quote: MAUVE correctly identifies the expected quality relationship, assigning the lowest quality to greedy decoding ( . 016) followed by ancestral sampling ( . 882), and the highest quality to nucleus sampling ( . 940).
comments.explanation: The text reports MAUVE for greedy decoding as 0.016, but Table 3 in the same section shows a value of 0.02 (the entry for 'Greedy' under MAUVE). The difference (0.004) is small but exceeds rounding expected from a single decimal place (0.016 vs 0.02). The tables are presumably from the same experiments; the discrepancy is not explained by rounding or table notes. A reader comparing the text claim with the table could be misled about the exact score. The ancestral (0.882 vs 0.88) and nucleus (0.940 vs 0.94) differences are consistent with truncation, but the greedy mismatch is larger.

comments.title: Repeated subheading 'MAUVE' may cause confusion
comments.quote:
## **4.1 Quantifying Properties of Generated Text**

...

**MAUVE** quality differences due to generation length.
...

**MAUVE** Recent work has identified two clear trends...
comments.explanation: The section contains two paragraph-level subheadings both labeled 'MAUVE' (one before the discussion of generation length, one before decoding algorithms). This appears to be a formatting artifact (possibly from the original PDF or OCR). While it does not affect the scientific content, a careful reader might momentarily think the text has switched topics or that a new subsection is beginning. The second 'MAUVE' is particularly awkward because it is followed directly by prose without a clear break. This is a minor readability issue.

comments.title: Ambiguity in human evaluation protocol: three rating dimensions not explained
comments.quote: In our study, a quality judgment means choosing a particular (model, decoder) setting based on the resultant generations.\n\n**Evaluation Protocol.** To obtain human judgments, we employ a pairwise setup: at each round, an annotator receives a context and continuations from two different (model, decoder) settings, and selects the continuation they found more natural using a 5-point Likert scale.
comments.explanation: The evaluation protocol described only mentions a single pairwise judgment of 'more natural'. Yet Table 5 reports correlations for three distinct dimensions: 'Human-like/BT', 'Interesting/BT', and 'Sensible/BT'. It is unclear whether these three dimensions were rated separately in the same experiment, whether different instructions were given, or whether they are derived post hoc from the single naturalness rating. The paper later refers to 'quality judgment' as a single concept, but the table implies multiple facet-specific human judgments. Without clarification, a reader cannot reproduce the protocol or understand what exactly was correlated. This is a methodological gap that affects the interpretability of the main correlation result.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a timely and methodologically sound contribution to the evaluation of open-ended text generation by introducing MAUVE, which leverages divergence frontiers to quantify the gap between model and human text distributions while capturing both precision-oriented (Type I) and diversity-oriented (Type II) errors. The motivation is well-articulated, and the use of mixture distributions to derive a scalar area-under-the-curve metric provides an interpretable and theoretically grounded improvement over existing heuristic-based or sparsity-sensitive measures. However, the paper would benefit from addressing significant concerns regarding the metric's dependencies and robustness: specifically, MAUVE's reliance on an external language model for embeddings introduces a confound where the score may reflect the alignment of the embedding space rather than the generative quality, necessitating a rigorous analysis of sensitivity to the choice of embedding model; additionally, the quantization step, while essential for tractability, raises concerns about information loss and hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., cluster count, binning strategy) that require deeper empirical validation beyond the claimed robustness. Finally, the clarity of the mathematical formulation would be enhanced by providing more precise definitions for the divergence estimator and the integration over $\lambda$ to ensure reproducibility, and the introduction should more explicitly discuss the computational overhead relative to the gains over simpler baselines.

comments.title: Contradiction between MAUVE definition and stated properties
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, **MAUVE** ( _P, Q_ ), is the area under the divergence curve _C_ ( _P, Q_ ). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) lies in (0 _,_ 1], with a larger value meaning that _Q_ is closer to _P_ . Further, MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) = 1 if and only if _Q_ = _P_ .
comments.explanation: The passage defines MAUVE as simply the area under the divergence curve. However, because KL divergences are non-negative, the raw area under the curve would be exactly zero when Q = P (since both coordinates are zero) and strictly positive otherwise. This directly contradicts the claim that MAUVE ∈ (0, 1] and that MAUVE = 1 if and only if Q = P. The authors must be applying an implicit normalization or transformation (e.g., MAUVE = 1 - normalized_area, or plotting scaled coordinates), but this step is completely omitted. Without it, a reader cannot reconcile the geometric definition with the stated range and optimality condition.

comments.title: Underspecified method due to missing equations
comments.quote:
This yields a _divergence curve_ ,

where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter for scaling.
comments.explanation: The actual mathematical formulation for the divergence curve is missing from the text. While this is partially an OCR artifact, the omission leaves the methodology underspecified. Specifically, the role of the scaling hyperparameter _c_ is never explained, and without the formula, it is impossible to understand how the curve coordinates are normalized or bounded. Similarly, the definition of the empirical quantized distribution _P̃_ is omitted in the adjacent paragraph. For a reader attempting to reproduce the metric, these missing steps are critical.

comments.title: Grammatical redundancy in literature review
comments.quote: Moreover, it has been recently shown in [42] show that these classical metrics only weakly agree with human judgments.
comments.explanation: The sentence contains a redundant verb phrase: "it has been recently shown in [42] show that". It should read either "it has been recently shown in [42] that" or "[42] show that". This is a minor proofreading error that does not impact the technical accuracy or mathematical content of the passage.

comments.title: Contradiction between MAUVE area definition and claimed score range
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, **MAUVE** ( _P, Q_ ), is the area under the divergence curve _C_ ( _P, Q_ ). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) lies in (0 _,_ 1], with a larger value meaning that _Q_ is closer to _P_ . Further, MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) = 1 if and only if _Q_ = _P_ .
comments.explanation: The divergence curve $C(P, Q)$ is defined using KL divergences $KL(Q\|R_\lambda)$ and $KL(P\|R_\lambda)$ as its coordinates. When $Q = P$, the mixture $R_\lambda = \lambda P + (1-\lambda)P = P$ for all $\lambda$, making both KL divergences identically zero. Consequently, the curve lies entirely at the origin $(0,0)$, and the area under it should be $0$. However, the text explicitly claims that MAUVE lies in $(0, 1]$ and equals $1$ if and only if $Q = P$. This is a direct mathematical contradiction under the stated definition of 'area under the divergence curve'. The authors likely normalize the axes (e.g., dividing by $c$) and define MAUVE as $1 - \text{Area}$ or compute the area between the curve and a reference line (like the top-right corner), but this crucial normalization or flipped definition is omitted. As written, a reader cannot reconcile the geometric definition with the scalar bounds, which undermines the metric's interpretation and reproducibility.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for human vs. model text samples across sections
comments.quote: Section 2: 'First, we sample human text _**x** i ⇠ P_ and machine text _**x**[0] i[⇠][Q]_.' vs Section 3: 'compares the perplexity of the model text _**x** ⇠ Q_ with that of human text _**x**[0] ⇠ P_ under an external model _R_ .'
comments.explanation: The notation assigning samples to the true ($P$) and model ($Q$) distributions is swapped between sections. In Section 2, $\mathbf{x}$ consistently denotes human text ($P$) and $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ denotes model text ($Q$). In Section 3, the roles are reversed, with $\mathbf{x} \sim Q$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} \sim P$. While this appears to be an author oversight rather than a single OCR error (as it spans distinct sections and reappears in Table 1), it creates unnecessary confusion. A careful reader implementing the divergence estimation or following the formal definitions could easily mix up which distribution generates which sample set, leading to incorrect KL divergence calculations or swapped expectations. Standardizing the notation is necessary for clarity.

comments.title: Underspecified scaling hyperparameter for the divergence curve
comments.quote:
This yields a _divergence curve_ , 
where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter for scaling.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a scaling hyperparameter $c > 0$ to define the divergence curve coordinates but fails to specify how $c$ is chosen. Since MAUVE is defined as the area under this scaled curve, the resulting score is highly sensitive to $c$. The paper claims the final score lies in $(0, 1]$, but provides no formula, rule, or normalization procedure for selecting $c$ to guarantee this range (e.g., setting $c$ to the maximum observed KL divergence, or normalizing the curve to the unit square). Without this specification, the metric is not fully reproducible, and the connection to the stated $(0, 1]$ bound remains mathematically unresolved. This likely interacts with the contradiction noted in Issue 1, as specifying $c$ and the area normalization would resolve the boundary conditions.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between text and Table 3 for generation perplexity comparison
comments.quote: generation perplexity rates the highly degenerate greedy-decoded text as better than ancestral sampling (11 _._ 324 vs. 19 _._ 284)
comments.explanation: The text claims that generation perplexity rates greedy decoding as better than ancestral sampling, citing values of 11.324 and 19.284 respectively. However, Table 3 (and the surrounding context) reports generation perplexity for the large GPT-2 model as 19.3 for greedy decoding and 1.5 for ancestral sampling. The cited values actually correspond to the adversarial decoder (11.3) and greedy decoding (19.3) rows in Table 3. Since lower perplexity is conventionally better, the authors' numbers and metric labels are mismatched. This appears to be a copy-paste or labeling error in the manuscript. While the broader claim that perplexity fails to capture generation quality degradation remains valid, the specific numerical evidence provided is incorrect and could mislead readers verifying the baseline comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous decoding criterion in adversarial sampling procedure
comments.quote: Adversarial perplexity sampling proceeds in two phases: (1) we generate the first 15% of tokens in a sequence uniformly at random from the vocabulary, and (2) we generate the remaining tokens greedily to make the running perplexity of the generated sequence as close as possible to the perplexity of human text.
comments.explanation: The procedure states that tokens are generated 'greedily' to minimize the gap between running perplexity and a target value. In language modeling literature, 'greedy decoding' strictly refers to selecting the highest-probability token at each step ($x_t = \arg\max \hat{P}(x_t|\mathbf{x}_{1:t})$). Here, the authors use 'greedily' to describe a token-selection optimization that minimizes $|\text{PPL}(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}) - \text{PPL}_{\text{human}}|$. This conflation of standard decoding terminology with a custom optimization objective is ambiguous and makes the method underspecified for reproduction. A careful reader might incorrectly implement standard greedy decoding or struggle to identify the exact scoring function used at each step. The description should explicitly state the selection criterion rather than relying on the overloaded term 'greedily'.

comments.title: Contradiction between divergence curve definition and claimed MAUVE range/interpretation
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, MAUVE(P, Q), is the area under the divergence curve C(P, Q). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE(P, Q) lies in (0, 1], with a larger value meaning that Q is closer to P. Further, MAUVE(P, Q) = 1 if and only if Q = P.
comments.explanation: The authors define the divergence curve C(P,Q) with coordinates based on KL divergences KL(Q||R_λ) and KL(P||R_λ). When Q = P, both KL divergences are identically zero for all λ, meaning the curve collapses to the origin (0,0). The area under such a curve would mathematically evaluate to 0. However, the text explicitly states that MAUVE lies in (0,1] and equals 1 exactly when Q = P. This creates a logical inconsistency: the raw area under the KL-divergence curve cannot simultaneously be 0 (at Q=P) and 1 (at Q=P). The authors likely apply a normalization or coordinate transformation (e.g., mapping the curve to a unit square or defining MAUVE as 1 minus the normalized area) to achieve the desired range and monotonic interpretation, but this crucial step is omitted from the passage. A careful reader following the formal definition would arrive at the opposite conclusion about the metric's behavior at Q=P.

comments.title: Awkward and grammatically incorrect phrasing in quantization robustness claim
comments.quote: The Spearman rank correlation between MAUVE computed with k-means for k ranging from 100 to 5000 correlates nearly perfectly with that of k = 500.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically redundant and logically imprecise. It reads as if the correlation itself 'correlates' with another correlation. The intended meaning is clearly that the MAUVE scores computed for each k in the range [100, 5000] are highly rank-correlated (Spearman ~0.99 or 1.00) with the scores computed using the default k=500. While the numerical claim is understandable and the result supports the authors' robustness conclusion, the phrasing obscures the statistical comparison being reported and could mislead a reader parsing the sentence literally.

comments.title: Minor typographical repetition
comments.quote: We compare different three different quantization algorithms:
comments.explanation: The word 'different' is accidentally repeated ('different three different'). This is a trivial editorial/typographical error that does not affect the technical content or reproducibility but is noticeable upon reading.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing and potentially misleading description of discriminator evaluation
comments.quote: We report the accuracy of Grover mega as the discriminator for the news generations as it produced the highest discrimination accuracy [61] while we use GPT-2 large for the story domain. As seen in Table 5, MAUVE correlates the highest with the discrimination accuracy (0 _._ 96 for news and 0 _._ 89 for stories) among all comparison measures. Computing the discrimination accuracy for each (model, decoder) pair requires fine-tuning a separate model, which is particularly expensive for large models such as Grover-mega.
comments.explanation: The opening phrase 'accuracy of Grover mega as the discriminator' is syntactically broken and reads as a drafting error; the intended meaning is almost certainly 'discrimination accuracy when using Grover Mega as the classifier'. More substantively, the claim that 'computing the discrimination accuracy for each (model, decoder) pair requires fine-tuning a separate model' introduces ambiguity about the evaluation protocol. Standard practice for this baseline metric involves fine-tuning a single binary classifier on a pooled dataset of real and generated samples from a domain, then evaluating its accuracy across different (model, decoder) pairs without retraining. The current wording could lead a careful reader to incorrectly assume the baseline requires distinct fine-tuning per experiment setting, which would be computationally prohibitive and non-standard. I first checked whether the authors meant to say the discriminator itself is expensive to train (which is plausible for large models), but the phrasing ties the cost directly to each '(model, decoder) pair', which contradicts typical evaluation setups. The authors' intent is clearly to contrast MAUVE's zero-training requirement with the baseline's computational overhead, but the description should clarify that the discriminator is trained once per domain/task. This is a minor framing/clarity issue that does not undermine the empirical results but could mislead reproducers.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a timely and methodologically sound contribution to the evaluation of open-ended text generation by introducing MAUVE, which leverages divergence frontiers to quantify the gap between model and human text distributions while capturing both precision-oriented (Type I) and diversity-oriented (Type II) errors. The motivation is well-articulated, and the use of mixture distributions to derive a scalar area-under-the-curve metric provides an interpretable and theoretically grounded improvement over existing heuristic-based or sparsity-sensitive measures. However, the paper would benefit from addressing significant concerns regarding the metric's dependencies and robustness: specifically, MAUVE's reliance on an external language model for embeddings introduces a confound where the score may reflect the alignment of the embedding space rather than the generative quality, necessitating a rigorous analysis of sensitivity to the choice of embedding model; additionally, the quantization step, while essential for tractability, raises concerns about information loss and hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., cluster count, binning strategy) that require deeper empirical validation beyond the claimed robustness. Finally, the clarity of the mathematical formulation would be enhanced by providing more precise definitions for the divergence estimator and the integration over $\lambda$ to ensure reproducibility, and the introduction should more explicitly discuss the computational overhead relative to the gains over simpler baselines.

comments.title: Contradiction between MAUVE definition and stated properties
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, **MAUVE** ( _P, Q_ ), is the area under the divergence curve _C_ ( _P, Q_ ). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) lies in (0 _,_ 1], with a larger value meaning that _Q_ is closer to _P_ . Further, MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) = 1 if and only if _Q_ = _P_ .
comments.explanation: The passage defines MAUVE as simply the area under the divergence curve. However, because KL divergences are non-negative, the raw area under the curve would be exactly zero when Q = P (since both coordinates are zero) and strictly positive otherwise. This directly contradicts the claim that MAUVE ∈ (0, 1] and that MAUVE = 1 if and only if Q = P. The authors must be applying an implicit normalization or transformation (e.g., MAUVE = 1 - normalized_area, or plotting scaled coordinates), but this step is completely omitted. Without it, a reader cannot reconcile the geometric definition with the stated range and optimality condition.

comments.title: Underspecified method due to missing equations
comments.quote:
This yields a _divergence curve_ ,

where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter for scaling.
comments.explanation: The actual mathematical formulation for the divergence curve is missing from the text. While this is partially an OCR artifact, the omission leaves the methodology underspecified. Specifically, the role of the scaling hyperparameter _c_ is never explained, and without the formula, it is impossible to understand how the curve coordinates are normalized or bounded. Similarly, the definition of the empirical quantized distribution _P̃_ is omitted in the adjacent paragraph. For a reader attempting to reproduce the metric, these missing steps are critical.

comments.title: Grammatical redundancy in literature review
comments.quote: Moreover, it has been recently shown in [42] show that these classical metrics only weakly agree with human judgments.
comments.explanation: The sentence contains a redundant verb phrase: "it has been recently shown in [42] show that". It should read either "it has been recently shown in [42] that" or "[42] show that". This is a minor proofreading error that does not impact the technical accuracy or mathematical content of the passage.

comments.title: Contradiction between MAUVE area definition and claimed score range
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, **MAUVE** ( _P, Q_ ), is the area under the divergence curve _C_ ( _P, Q_ ). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) lies in (0 _,_ 1], with a larger value meaning that _Q_ is closer to _P_ . Further, MAUVE( _P, Q_ ) = 1 if and only if _Q_ = _P_ .
comments.explanation: The divergence curve $C(P, Q)$ is defined using KL divergences $KL(Q\|R_\lambda)$ and $KL(P\|R_\lambda)$ as its coordinates. When $Q = P$, the mixture $R_\lambda = \lambda P + (1-\lambda)P = P$ for all $\lambda$, making both KL divergences identically zero. Consequently, the curve lies entirely at the origin $(0,0)$, and the area under it should be $0$. However, the text explicitly claims that MAUVE lies in $(0, 1]$ and equals $1$ if and only if $Q = P$. This is a direct mathematical contradiction under the stated definition of 'area under the divergence curve'. The authors likely normalize the axes (e.g., dividing by $c$) and define MAUVE as $1 - \text{Area}$ or compute the area between the curve and a reference line (like the top-right corner), but this crucial normalization or flipped definition is omitted. As written, a reader cannot reconcile the geometric definition with the scalar bounds, which undermines the metric's interpretation and reproducibility.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for human vs. model text samples across sections
comments.quote: Section 2: 'First, we sample human text _**x** i ⇠ P_ and machine text _**x**[0] i[⇠][Q]_.' vs Section 3: 'compares the perplexity of the model text _**x** ⇠ Q_ with that of human text _**x**[0] ⇠ P_ under an external model _R_ .'
comments.explanation: The notation assigning samples to the true ($P$) and model ($Q$) distributions is swapped between sections. In Section 2, $\mathbf{x}$ consistently denotes human text ($P$) and $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ denotes model text ($Q$). In Section 3, the roles are reversed, with $\mathbf{x} \sim Q$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} \sim P$. While this appears to be an author oversight rather than a single OCR error (as it spans distinct sections and reappears in Table 1), it creates unnecessary confusion. A careful reader implementing the divergence estimation or following the formal definitions could easily mix up which distribution generates which sample set, leading to incorrect KL divergence calculations or swapped expectations. Standardizing the notation is necessary for clarity.

comments.title: Underspecified scaling hyperparameter for the divergence curve
comments.quote:
This yields a _divergence curve_ , 
where _c >_ 0 is a hyperparameter for scaling.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a scaling hyperparameter $c > 0$ to define the divergence curve coordinates but fails to specify how $c$ is chosen. Since MAUVE is defined as the area under this scaled curve, the resulting score is highly sensitive to $c$. The paper claims the final score lies in $(0, 1]$, but provides no formula, rule, or normalization procedure for selecting $c$ to guarantee this range (e.g., setting $c$ to the maximum observed KL divergence, or normalizing the curve to the unit square). Without this specification, the metric is not fully reproducible, and the connection to the stated $(0, 1]$ bound remains mathematically unresolved. This likely interacts with the contradiction noted in Issue 1, as specifying $c$ and the area normalization would resolve the boundary conditions.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between text and Table 3 for generation perplexity comparison
comments.quote: generation perplexity rates the highly degenerate greedy-decoded text as better than ancestral sampling (11 _._ 324 vs. 19 _._ 284)
comments.explanation: The text claims that generation perplexity rates greedy decoding as better than ancestral sampling, citing values of 11.324 and 19.284 respectively. However, Table 3 (and the surrounding context) reports generation perplexity for the large GPT-2 model as 19.3 for greedy decoding and 1.5 for ancestral sampling. The cited values actually correspond to the adversarial decoder (11.3) and greedy decoding (19.3) rows in Table 3. Since lower perplexity is conventionally better, the authors' numbers and metric labels are mismatched. This appears to be a copy-paste or labeling error in the manuscript. While the broader claim that perplexity fails to capture generation quality degradation remains valid, the specific numerical evidence provided is incorrect and could mislead readers verifying the baseline comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous decoding criterion in adversarial sampling procedure
comments.quote: Adversarial perplexity sampling proceeds in two phases: (1) we generate the first 15% of tokens in a sequence uniformly at random from the vocabulary, and (2) we generate the remaining tokens greedily to make the running perplexity of the generated sequence as close as possible to the perplexity of human text.
comments.explanation: The procedure states that tokens are generated 'greedily' to minimize the gap between running perplexity and a target value. In language modeling literature, 'greedy decoding' strictly refers to selecting the highest-probability token at each step ($x_t = \arg\max \hat{P}(x_t|\mathbf{x}_{1:t})$). Here, the authors use 'greedily' to describe a token-selection optimization that minimizes $|\text{PPL}(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}) - \text{PPL}_{\text{human}}|$. This conflation of standard decoding terminology with a custom optimization objective is ambiguous and makes the method underspecified for reproduction. A careful reader might incorrectly implement standard greedy decoding or struggle to identify the exact scoring function used at each step. The description should explicitly state the selection criterion rather than relying on the overloaded term 'greedily'.

comments.title: Contradiction between divergence curve definition and claimed MAUVE range/interpretation
comments.quote: Our proposed measure, MAUVE(P, Q), is the area under the divergence curve C(P, Q). It provides a scalar summary of the trade-off between Type I and Type II errors. MAUVE(P, Q) lies in (0, 1], with a larger value meaning that Q is closer to P. Further, MAUVE(P, Q) = 1 if and only if Q = P.
comments.explanation: The authors define the divergence curve C(P,Q) with coordinates based on KL divergences KL(Q||R_λ) and KL(P||R_λ). When Q = P, both KL divergences are identically zero for all λ, meaning the curve collapses to the origin (0,0). The area under such a curve would mathematically evaluate to 0. However, the text explicitly states that MAUVE lies in (0,1] and equals 1 exactly when Q = P. This creates a logical inconsistency: the raw area under the KL-divergence curve cannot simultaneously be 0 (at Q=P) and 1 (at Q=P). The authors likely apply a normalization or coordinate transformation (e.g., mapping the curve to a unit square or defining MAUVE as 1 minus the normalized area) to achieve the desired range and monotonic interpretation, but this crucial step is omitted from the passage. A careful reader following the formal definition would arrive at the opposite conclusion about the metric's behavior at Q=P.

comments.title: Awkward and grammatically incorrect phrasing in quantization robustness claim
comments.quote: The Spearman rank correlation between MAUVE computed with k-means for k ranging from 100 to 5000 correlates nearly perfectly with that of k = 500.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically redundant and logically imprecise. It reads as if the correlation itself 'correlates' with another correlation. The intended meaning is clearly that the MAUVE scores computed for each k in the range [100, 5000] are highly rank-correlated (Spearman ~0.99 or 1.00) with the scores computed using the default k=500. While the numerical claim is understandable and the result supports the authors' robustness conclusion, the phrasing obscures the statistical comparison being reported and could mislead a reader parsing the sentence literally.

comments.title: Minor typographical repetition
comments.quote: We compare different three different quantization algorithms:
comments.explanation: The word 'different' is accidentally repeated ('different three different'). This is a trivial editorial/typographical error that does not affect the technical content or reproducibility but is noticeable upon reading.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing and potentially misleading description of discriminator evaluation
comments.quote: We report the accuracy of Grover mega as the discriminator for the news generations as it produced the highest discrimination accuracy [61] while we use GPT-2 large for the story domain. As seen in Table 5, MAUVE correlates the highest with the discrimination accuracy (0 _._ 96 for news and 0 _._ 89 for stories) among all comparison measures. Computing the discrimination accuracy for each (model, decoder) pair requires fine-tuning a separate model, which is particularly expensive for large models such as Grover-mega.
comments.explanation: The opening phrase 'accuracy of Grover mega as the discriminator' is syntactically broken and reads as a drafting error; the intended meaning is almost certainly 'discrimination accuracy when using Grover Mega as the classifier'. More substantively, the claim that 'computing the discrimination accuracy for each (model, decoder) pair requires fine-tuning a separate model' introduces ambiguity about the evaluation protocol. Standard practice for this baseline metric involves fine-tuning a single binary classifier on a pooled dataset of real and generated samples from a domain, then evaluating its accuracy across different (model, decoder) pairs without retraining. The current wording could lead a careful reader to incorrectly assume the baseline requires distinct fine-tuning per experiment setting, which would be computationally prohibitive and non-standard. I first checked whether the authors meant to say the discriminator itself is expensive to train (which is plausible for large models), but the phrasing ties the cost directly to each '(model, decoder) pair', which contradicts typical evaluation setups. The authors' intent is clearly to contrast MAUVE's zero-training requirement with the baseline's computational overhead, but the description should clarify that the discriminator is trained once per domain/task. This is a minor framing/clarity issue that does not undermine the empirical results but could mislead reproducers.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The introduction to this paper presents a sophisticated and rigorously motivated framework for addressing a critical challenge in natural language processing: evaluating the distributional alignment between machine-generated and human-written text. The authors introduce MAUVE, a theoretically grounded metric that builds upon information divergence frontiers to differentiate between Type I errors (false positives, such as degenerate text) and Type II errors (coverage deficits), providing a more nuanced evaluation of open-ended generation than standard benchmarks or perplexity. The writing is exceptionally clear, effectively translating complex statistical concepts like mixture distributions and quantized embeddings into a practical interface for researchers. While the empirical implications of the proposed method remain to be fully validated in the subsequent sections, the theoretical formulation presents a significant contribution by bridging the gap between discrete language distributions and computable low-dimensional measures.

comments.title: Grammatical Typo in Citation Phrase
comments.quote: Moreover, it has been recently shown in [42] show that these classical metrics only weakly agree with human judgments.
comments.explanation: The phrase "shown in [42] show" contains a grammatical error. It is likely that the author intended to say either "shown in [42]" or "in [42] show" (repeating the citation but omitting 'shown'), or perhaps "it has been recently shown that [42] shows...". As written, the word 'shown' and the preposition 'in' create a redundant and incorrect phrasing. While the meaning is clear to a reader, the syntax is technically incorrect.

comments.title: Grammatical Fragment in Experiments Introduction
comments.quote: We perform three sets of experiments to validate MAUVE. 4.1) examine how known properties of generated text with respect to generation length, decoding algorithm, and model size can be identified and quantified by MAUVE.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure is incomplete and grammatically disjointed. It states 'We perform three sets of experiments...' but then presents the section title and topic '4.1) examine' as a fragment rather than a complete clause ('Section 4.1 examines...' or 'Section 4.1, which examines...'). While context clearly indicates this refers to Section 4.1, the phrasing '4.1) examine' is a dangling modifier or a typographical slip.

comments.title: Inconsistent Data Alignment in Table 5
comments.quote: Table 5 shows the correlation between human judgments and five automatic evaluation metrics...
comments.explanation: Upon examining Table 5, the numerical values in the columns corresponding to the automatic metrics (e.g., Gen. PPL, Zipf Coef., REP) are identical to the Bradley-Terry preference scores (the first column of data, e.g., 0.810). For instance, the row 'Human-like/BT | Web text' lists '0.810' under 'Gen. PPL', '0.833' under 'Zipf Coef.', etc. However, the text introduces these columns as containing the metric values (or potentially their correlations). Since the numeric values in the metric columns (0.810, 0.833...) are exactly the same as the preference scores listed in the first data column, it appears that the column headers and data are misaligned or that the metric values were incorrectly copied from the preference coefficients. This creates significant confusion for a reader trying to extract the actual metric scores or correlations from the table.

comments.title: OCR-induced Formatting Errors in Numeric Values
comments.explanation: Multiple instances of OCR artifacts are present in the passage, specifically underscores preceding decimal points (e.g., '0_._ 993'). These clearly represent typos in the PDF extraction intended to be standard decimal points ('0.993'). While not strictly mathematical or logical errors in the author's derivation, these formatting issues disrupt the reading flow and suggest potential data loss or noise in the text extraction process.

comments.title: Underspecified DRMM Training Procedure
comments.quote: We convert the soft clustering returned by DRMM into a hard clustering... We use DRMM with 3 layers and 10 components per layer... train it for 20 epochs.
comments.explanation: In the discussion of quantization methods, the authors introduce a Deep Residual Mixture Model (DRMM) and state that it is trained ('train it for 20 epochs') to create the histograms for MAUVE calculation. While the quantization is described as 'jointly quantizing' samples, the explicit mention of training a generative model on the evaluation corpus is a methodological detail that warrants clarification regarding on-sample bias. If the DRMM is trained on the exact samples used for the MAUVE calculation (human and generated), the resulting metric might be optimized for those specific distributions rather than being a general 'quantization' strategy. The comparison to 'k-means' (which is usually non-parametric relative to the sample distribution, just grouping points) becomes questionable if DRMM effectively 'learns' the clusters from the data in a way that overfits the specific sample composition.

comments.title: Direction of Correlation Contradiction
comments.quote: As seen in Table 5, MAUVE correlates the highest with the discrimination accuracy (0.96 for news and 0.89 for stories) among all comparison measures.
comments.explanation: The authors state that 'a lower discrimination accuracy implies that the generation is harder to distinguish from human text' (i.e., better generation). Since MAUVE measures the closeness of generation to human text (higher is better), it should correlate *negatively* with discrimination accuracy (higher is better = worse generation). A reported correlation coefficient of +0.96 implies a positive relationship. If the correlation is truly +0.96, it would mean that higher MAUVE (better generation) is associated with higher discrimination accuracy (worse generation), which is logically contradictory to the text's definition of the metric. It is possible the authors correlated with 'Discriminability' (1 - Accuracy) but labeled the metric 'accuracy' in the text, or the reported correlation coefficient is missing a minus sign. This undermines the specific empirical claim about the relationship between MAUVE and discriminators.

comments.title: Missing Hyphen in Model Name
comments.quote: We report the accuracy of Grover mega as the discriminator...
comments.explanation: The text references 'Grover mega' without a hyphen, while earlier in the context (Section 4 and Table 2) the model is consistently referred to as 'Grover' or 'Grover-mega'. This appears to be either an OCR error (common with dash symbols) or a minor typo. While resolvable, it disrupts the consistent notation established earlier in the paper.
